steps.prompt: Which is the main verb of the first clause?

hints.seducamini: 'Seducamini' is a subjunctive verb in the subordinate clause after 'ne'
hints.venient: 'Venient' is a finite verb but belongs to the second clause

hints.to avoid: 'To avoid' is 'vitare' — 'Videte' comes from 'videre' (to see/watch out)

steps.reveal: Videte = Watch out! See to it! (2nd pl. imperative)

hints.present indicative: Present indicative 2nd plural would be 'videtis' — 'Videte' is imperative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive 2nd plural would be 'videatis' — 'Videte' is imperative
hints.future indicative: Future 2nd plural would be 'videbitis' — 'Videte' is imperative

hints.vinco, vincere, vici, victum: These are the principal parts of 'vinco' (to conquer)
hints.vivo, vivere, vixi, victum: These are the principal parts of 'vivo' (to live)
hints.vito, vitare, vitavi, vitatum: These are the principal parts of 'vito' (to avoid)

steps.reveal: seducamini = you be led astray (pres. pass. subj.)

hints.present passive indicative: Present passive indicative 2nd pl. would be 'seducimini' — the -a- marks subjunctive
hints.imperfect passive subjunctive: Imperfect passive subjunctive would be 'seduceremini' — 'seducamini' is present

hints.duco, ducere, duxi, ductum: These are the parts of 'duco' (to lead) — 'seducere' is the compound se- + ducere
hints.sequor, sequi, secutus sum, —: These are the parts of 'sequor' (to follow) — a deponent verb
hints.sedeo, sedere, sedi, sessum: These are the parts of 'sedeo' (to sit) — different root from 'seducere'

steps.target: 'ne' + subjunctive expresses a negative purpose/warning clause
choices: 'ne' + subjunctive expresses a negative purpose/warning clause
steps.reveal: Videte ne seducamini = See to it that you are not led astray

hints.it is an indirect question: Indirect questions use interrogative words (quis, quid, etc.), not 'ne' after 'videte'
hints.it is a result clause: Result clauses use 'ut/ut non' with subjunctive, not 'ne' after an imperative of warning
hints.it is a temporal clause: Temporal clauses use 'cum/dum/postquam' — 'ne' here signals a warning clause

steps.reveal: venient = they will come (3rd pl. fut.)

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'veniunt' — 'venient' with -ie- is future
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'veniant' — 'venient' is future indicative

hints.vendo, vendere, vendidi, venditum: These are the parts of 'vendo' (to sell)
hints.vinco, vincere, vici, victum: These are the parts of 'vinco' (to conquer)
hints.verto, vertere, verti, versum: These are the parts of 'verto' (to turn)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'nomen' — 'nomine' with -e ending is ablative
hints.vocative: Vocative would be 'nomen' — 'nomine' is ablative after 'in'
hints.dative: Dative would be 'nomini' — 'nomine' is ablative governed by 'in'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'nominis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'nominis' ends in -is
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'nominis' ends in -is

translation: Watch out that you are not led astray, for many will come in my name
